architecture.summary: SATNet is a differentiable MAXSAT solver layer that integrates into deep neural networks. The forward pass relaxes discrete MAXSAT variables into continuous vectors via randomized rounding, solves a semidefinite program (SDP) relaxation using block coordinate descent to compute output variable assignments, and converts continuous relaxations to probabilistic outputs. The backward pass analytically differentiates through the SDP solution using a mirrored coordinate descent procedure to compute gradients with respect to inputs and learnable weight matrix. The layer can be combined with standard neural components (CNNs, LSTMs) for end-to-end training.
trained_components: MAXSAT SDP weight matrix (low-rank coefficient matrix W)

biblio.title: SATNet: Bridging deep learning and logical reasoning using a differentiable satisfiability solver

claims.statement: On Sudoku task, SATNet achieves 98.3% board-wise test accuracy
On permuted Sudoku (boards with fixed bit permutation applied), SATNet achieves same 98.3% board-wise test accuracy
On parity function learning with single-bit supervision, SATNet converges to zero test error within 20 epochs for input sequences of length 50 and 150
On visual Sudoku (MNIST digit images mapped to logical solutions), SATNet achieves 63.2% test accuracy, reaching 85% of theoretical best (74.7%)
Sudoku experiments: SATNet reaches 95.0% test accuracy after 22 epochs (37 minutes on GTX 1080 Ti GPU) and 98.3% after 100 epochs (172 minutes)

The forward pass uses block coordinate descent to solve an SDP relaxation of MAXSAT with per-iteration runtime O(rank × m) where m is number of clauses, and only a small number of iterations is required for convergence
GPU CUDA-C implementation is up to x faster than corresponding OpenMP implementation on Xeon CPUs (for parallelization of rank-one updates in coordinate descent)
OptNet model made little progress on Sudoku problem even after a few days of training
